questions.text: In the context of the GIRES software failure, which factor most critically undermined the project's ability to recover despite multiple corrective interventions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insufficient initial budget allocation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Technological obsolescence of the stack

choices.label: C
choices.text: Uncontrolled scope expansion resulting from misaligned requirements

choices.label: D
choices.text: Lack of developer proficiency in the primary programming language

questions.explanation: While cost and quality were issues, the core strategic failure of GIRES was constant scope creep (requirements changes) which made the target impossible to hit, regardless of budget or technical skill.

questions.text: Analyze the following statement: "The system shall allow users to process payments in under \( 3 \) seconds." Which requirement type does this primarily serve, and why?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Business Requirement; it defines the strategic competitive advantage.

choices.label: B
choices.text: User Requirement; it focuses on the end-user's task completion speed.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Non-functional (Performance) Requirement; it constrains the operational quality of a function.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Process Requirement; it dictates how the developer must write the code.

questions.explanation: Performance requirements are a subset of non-functional requirements that quantify the efficiency or throughput of the system's functions.

questions.text: During a project audit, it is discovered that \( 15 \% \) of the specified system features do not map back to any defined business objectives. According to standard Requirements Engineering principles, what is the most appropriate action?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Keep them as 'Gold Plating' to enhance user satisfaction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Eliminate them to prevent scope creep and resource waste.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Renegotiate the business objectives to include these features.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Implement them only if the developers have extra time at the end.

questions.explanation: Requirements traceability requires that every technical requirement justifies its existence by fulfilling a business objective. Features without such mapping should be eliminated to maintain project focus.

questions.text: A software team identifies that the system's memory leaks are caused by a specific third-party library's interaction with the garbage collector. This conclusion is an outcome of which process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Scope definition

choices.label: B
choices.text: Root cause analysis

choices.label: C
choices.text: Validation testing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Feasibility study

questions.explanation: Root cause analysis goes beyond symptoms (memory leaks) to find the underlying origin (library/GC interaction) to ensure the problem is solved at its source.

questions.text: Which of the following activities is specifically categorized under the 'Elicitation' phase of Requirements Engineering rather than the 'Analysis' phase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Identifying and mapping stakeholders to their interest profiles

choices.label: B
choices.text: Checking for logical contradictions between user and system requirements

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prioritizing requirements based on cost-benefit ratios

choices.label: D
choices.text: Constructing a formal model of the system's data flow

questions.explanation: Stakeholder identification and information gathering (sources) are elicitation tasks. Contradiction checking and prioritization are analysis/negotiation tasks.

questions.text: In a software engineering environment, if \( 80 \% \) of the effort is directed toward communication and management, what is the primary risk of attempting to reduce this to \( 50 \% \) to increase 'technical' coding time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The software will have higher technical complexity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased likelihood of building a system that doesn't meet user needs due to information silos.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased portability between different operating systems.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The project will be completed ahead of schedule with fewer bugs.

questions.explanation: Because modern software success depends heavily on stakeholder alignment, reducing communication effort usually leads to requirements misalignment and project failure.

questions.text: A company is developing a new mobile banking app for global consumers. Since there is no specific client to interview, who should the Requirements Engineer treat as the primary proxy for requirements validation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The lead developer

choices.label: B
choices.text: A regulatory compliance officer

choices.label: C
choices.text: A market specialist with consumer data

choices.label: D
choices.text: The UI/UX designer

questions.explanation: For mass-market software (COTS), marketing specialists act as proxies for the user base to ensure the product meets general public needs.

questions.text: While conducting a requirements session, an engineer identifies a conflict between the Finance Department's security needs and the Sales Department's ease-of-use needs. Moving to resolve this through compromise is part of which sub-process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Elicitation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Specification

choices.label: C
choices.text: Negotiation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Validation

questions.explanation: Negotiation is the phase specifically dedicated to resolving conflicts discovered during analysis and elicitation through stakeholder agreement.

questions.text: Why is a strictly sequential (waterfall) approach to requirements elicitation considered high-risk in complex software projects?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It requires too much documentation in the early stages.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It assumes that stakeholders have perfect knowledge of their needs at the start.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It prevents the use of modern programming languages like Python.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It makes the resulting software impossible to test.

questions.explanation: Complex projects require an iterative approach because stakeholder needs evolve and their understanding of the system's potential changes as they see prototypes.

questions.text: Which of the following would be found in a Product **Vision** document rather than a Project **Scope** document?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The maximum number of users the database can support in version 1.0.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The long-term transformation of the company into a leader of AI-driven logistics.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A list of third-party APIs that will be excluded from the current sprint.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Detailed hardware specifications for the initial server deployment.

questions.explanation: Vision is high-level and long-term, potentially spanning multiple projects/releases. Scope is specific to what is included in a defined development effort.

questions.text: A critical system has a Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) of \( 1,000 \) hours and a Mean Time To Repair (MTTR) of \( 2 \) hours. What is the calculated availability metric for this reliability requirement?

choices.label: C
choices.text: \( 500 \) hours

questions.explanation: Availability = \( \frac{MTTF}{MTTF + MTTR} = \frac{1000}{1002} \approx 0.9980 \) or \( 99.80 \% \).

questions.text: A developer argues that using a proprietary, OS-specific framework will speed up development, but it increases the percentage of target-dependent statements to \( 45 \% \). Which quality attribute is being sacrificed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Maintainability

choices.label: B
choices.text: Robustness

choices.label: C
choices.text: Portability

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reliability

questions.explanation: Portability is inversely related to the number of target-dependent statements in the code.

questions.text: Consider the business rule: 'All customer data must be encrypted using AES-256 standards to comply with GDPR.' This rule generates which types of requirements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only Functional requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only Non-functional (Security) requirements.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both Functional (the act of encryption) and Non-functional (the specific standard and legal constraint).

choices.label: D
choices.text: None; business rules are not used in software requirements.

questions.explanation: Business rules are rich sources that dictate both what the system does (Function) and the constraints/standards it must follow (NFR).

questions.text: If a software defect costs \( \$ 100 \) to fix during the Specification phase, based on NIST trends, what is the approximate cost of fixing that same defect if it is not discovered until the Maintenance phase?
questions.explanation: The cost of fixing errors increases exponentially as the project progresses. A defect found in maintenance can cost 100x more than one found in the specification phase.

questions.text: A project charter specifies that 'All source code must be peer-reviewed and pass a static analysis check before merging into the main branch.' This is an example of:

choices.label: A
choices.text: A Performance Requirement

choices.label: B
choices.text: A Process Requirement

choices.label: C
choices.text: A Commercial Constraint

choices.label: D
choices.text: A Functional Requirement

questions.explanation: Process requirements constrain the 'how' of development—the methods, standards, and procedures used by the team.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of 'Requirements Validation' rather than 'Requirements Verification'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensuring the specification document is formatted correctly according to IEEE standards.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Confirming with the stakeholder that the documented 'search' function actually solves their business problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Running a spell-checker on the requirements document.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Checking if the system requirements are consistent with the user requirements.

questions.explanation: Validation asks: 'Are we building the right system?' (Does it meet user needs?). Verification asks: 'Are we building the system right?' (Does it meet the spec?).

questions.text: In a requirements meeting, the client says: "I want the system to be fast and user-friendly." The engineer fails to ask for quantifiable metrics for 'fast'. This leads to which category of problem?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Problem of Scope

choices.label: B
choices.text: Problem of Volatility

choices.label: C
choices.text: Problem of Understanding (Ambiguity)

choices.label: D
choices.text: Problem of Budget

questions.explanation: Vague terms like 'fast' or 'user-friendly' are problems of understanding. They require clarification and quantification to become actionable system requirements.

questions.text: A user requirement states: 'The system must allow a manager to approve an expense report.' Which of the following is a derived technical **system requirement** for this?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The manager should be happy with the interface.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The system shall provide a digital signature field with 2048-bit RSA encryption for the approval action.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Expenses are important to track for tax reasons.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Managers must have a computer to use the system.

questions.explanation: System requirements translate high-level user needs into detailed technical specifications that a developer can implement.

questions.text: During 'The Storm' phase of a brainstorming session for a new e-commerce feature, a participant suggests: 'The system should allow users to teleport their items instantly.' How should the facilitator respond?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reject the idea immediately as it is technically impossible.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Record the idea without criticism to encourage free flow of thought.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ask the participant to explain the physics of teleportation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stop the meeting and check the project's budget.

questions.explanation: In 'The Storm' phase of brainstorming, the goal is quantity and creativity. Evaluation and filtering only happen during 'The Calm'.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the use of a 'Scenario' in requirements discovery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A mathematical proof of software correctness.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A narrative description of a specific user interaction with the system, including error handling and state changes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A high-level diagram showing the database tables.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A contract signed by the client stating they will not change requirements.

questions.explanation: Scenarios provide concrete, real-world examples of system usage, helping to uncover edge cases and exceptions.

questions.text: Joint Application Development (JAD) is most effective for solving which of the following problems?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Writing the actual source code for the backend.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reaching consensus among diverse stakeholders with conflicting priorities in a short time.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determining the MTTF of a hardware component.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reducing the total cost of server hosting.

questions.explanation: JAD/Workshops bring everyone together to force decisions and resolve conflicts through facilitated face-to-face interaction.

questions.text: A project suffers from 'Tacit Knowledge' issues. What does this imply about the requirements elicitation process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The requirements are stored in an encrypted database.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stakeholders assume certain requirements are so obvious they don't need to be mentioned.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The developer refuses to share the source code.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The client is lying about the project's budget.

questions.explanation: Tacit knowledge refers to 'what users know but cannot easily articulate' or what they assume is 'common sense' that the developer should already know.

questions.text: The 'I'll Know It When I See It' (IKIWISI) syndrome is best mitigated by which of the following engineering practices?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Writing more detailed 500-page specification documents.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Strictly enforcing a 'no-change' policy after the first month.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Rapid prototyping and incremental delivery of working software.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hiring more experienced manual testers.

questions.explanation: Prototyping allows users to interact with a concrete representation of the system early, helping them refine their requirements.

questions.text:
Construct a logical sequence for the Requirements Engineering process using the following phases: 
1. Validation 
2. Elicitation 
3. Specification 
4. Analysis & Negotiation

choices.label: A
choices.text: 2 \(\rightarrow\) 3 \(\rightarrow\) 4 \(\rightarrow\) 1

choices.label: B
choices.text: 2 \(\rightarrow\) 4 \(\rightarrow\) 3 \(\rightarrow\) 1

choices.label: C
choices.text: 4 \(\rightarrow\) 2 \(\rightarrow\) 1 \(\rightarrow\) 3

choices.label: D
choices.text: 3 \(\rightarrow\) 1 \(\rightarrow\) 2 \(\rightarrow\) 4

questions.explanation: The standard flow is Elicitation (discovery), Analysis & Negotiation (conflict resolution), Specification (documentation), and Validation (final check).

questions.text: A project fails because the 'System Boundary' was never clearly defined, leading to the team trying to solve problems that were actually outside the software's intended responsibility. This is a:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Problem of Understanding

choices.label: B
choices.text: Problem of Volatility

choices.label: C
choices.text: Problem of Scope

choices.label: D
choices.text: Problem of Portability

questions.explanation: Scope problems involve ill-defined system boundaries where it is unclear what is 'in' and what is 'out' of the system's responsibility.

questions.text: Which attribute of a 'good' requirement is violated if a requirement states: 'The system should be implemented using either Java or C++, depending on what the intern prefers'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Traceability

choices.label: B
choices.text: Verifiability

choices.label: C
choices.text: Unambiguousness / Definitive choice

choices.label: D
choices.text: Portability

questions.explanation: Requirements must be definitive and unambiguous to guide the development process. Leaving core technical choices to 'preference' creates uncertainty.

questions.text: During Requirements Management, a 'Change Control Board' is primarily responsible for:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Writing the code for new requirements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evaluating the impact, cost, and risk of proposed changes to the requirements baseline.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Marketing the new features to the general public.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Disciplining developers who introduce bugs.

questions.explanation: Requirements Management involves handling the evolution of requirements through a formal change control process.

questions.text: Which metric would best help a manager determine if the 'Requirements Volatility' is decreasing as the project nears the end of the specification phase?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of lines of code written per day.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The rate of change in the requirements document (additions/deletions per week).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The CPU usage of the development server.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The MTTF of the current prototype.

questions.explanation: Volatility is measured by the frequency and volume of changes to requirements. A stabilized document indicates low volatility.

questions.text: Which scenario best illustrates a 'Problem of Volatility'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The user uses the term 'database' to refer to an Excel spreadsheet.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The client changes the payment processing logic three times during the analysis phase because of shifting market regulations.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The developer realizes the chosen server cannot handle the traffic load.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The stakeholders forget to mention they need the app to work in offline mode.

questions.explanation: Volatility refers to requirements that change over time, often due to external factors like regulations or shifting business needs.

questions.text: According to research on software project failures (like Standish Group or NIST), which of these is the leading root cause?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The use of obsolete hardware.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Poorly defined or incomplete requirements and lack of user involvement.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Choosing a programming language that is too difficult.

choices.label: D
choices.text: High turnover of junior developers.

questions.explanation: Requirements-related issues are consistently cited as the primary reason for project failure, overrun, or cancellation.

questions.text: A 'Non-functional Requirement' specifies that 'The system must be compliant with Section 508 accessibility guidelines.' This is primarily a constraint on:

choices.label: A
choices.text: System performance

choices.label: B
choices.text: Usability and Legal Compliance

choices.label: C
choices.text: Database normalization

choices.label: D
choices.text: Algorithm efficiency

questions.explanation: Accessibility and legal standards are constraints on the system's operational characteristics and usability.

questions.text: Which phase of Requirements Engineering is most likely to produce a formal SRS (Software Requirements Specification) document?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inception

choices.label: B
choices.text: Elicitation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Specification

choices.label: D
choices.text: Negotiation

questions.explanation: Specification is the phase dedicated to documenting the requirements in a formal or semi-formal manner (the SRS).

questions.text: When comparing 'Interviews' and 'Focus Groups', what is the distinct advantage of a Focus Group for requirements gathering?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It provides a private space for stakeholders to share sensitive information.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It allows for the observation of group dynamics and identifies shared needs through cross-pollination of ideas.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is easier to schedule than a single interview.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It eliminates the need for a moderator.

questions.explanation: Focus groups leverage the interaction between multiple participants to discover collective requirements that might not emerge in one-on-one sessions.

questions.text: A software engineer uses a Graphviz diagram to model the Requirements Engineering process. Which diagram correctly represents the cyclic/iterative nature of these activities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: ```dot\ndigraph G { Elicitation -> Analysis -> Specification -> Validation -> Elicitation }\n```

choices.label: B
choices.text: ```dot\ndigraph G { Coding -> Testing -> Deployment }\n```

choices.label: C
choices.text: ```dot\ndigraph G { Start -> End }\n```

choices.label: D
choices.text: ```dot\ndigraph G { Elicitation -> Specification }\n```

questions.explanation: Requirements engineering is inherently iterative; validation often reveals the need for more elicitation or analysis.

questions.text: In a brainstorming session, 'The Calm' phase is used to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ensure everyone takes a break to reduce stress.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Systematically prune, group, and rank the ideas generated in the 'Storm' phase.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Assign blame for the most unrealistic ideas.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Write the final code for the selected ideas.

questions.explanation: The 'Calm' is the convergent phase where the team filters and prioritizes the 'wild' ideas into a realistic set of requirements.

questions.text: A requirement states: 'The system should be easy to learn.' How can this be transformed into a verifiable System Requirement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By adding a note: 'The system must be intuitive.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: By specifying: 'A user with basic computer skills shall be able to perform a core task in under 5 minutes without a manual.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: By requiring the developers to attend a usability seminar.

choices.label: D
choices.text: By stating: 'Users will like the system.'

questions.explanation: Verifiable requirements must be quantifiable. Setting a time-to-task metric for a specific user profile makes 'easy to learn' testable.

questions.text: Which of the following describes a 'Process Constraint' rather than a 'Product Constraint'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The application must run on Android and iOS.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The system must use an Agile Scrum methodology with 2-week sprints.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The database must support \( 10,000 \) transactions per second.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The login screen must include a 'forgot password' link.

questions.explanation: Process constraints limit how the software is built (methodology, tools), while product constraints limit the software itself (performance, platform).

questions.text: What is the primary danger of having a 'Market Specialist' as the only source of requirements for a COTS (Commercial Off-The-Shelf) product?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They might focus on 'sellability' and features over technical robustness and realistic feasibility.

choices.label: B
choices.text: They don't know how to use a computer.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are too expensive to hire.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They will try to write the code themselves.

questions.explanation: Marketing specialists focus on market trends and features that sell products, which may conflict with technical reality or long-term maintainability.

questions.text: The concept of 'Requirements Traceability' is essential because it allows an engineer to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: Track which developer wrote which line of code.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trace a business need to its corresponding system requirement, and then to the specific test case that validates it.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Determine the exact date the project will be finished.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Calculate the electricity cost of the servers.

questions.explanation: Traceability ensures that every requirement is justified by a business need and verified by a test, closing the loop of the engineering process.

questions.text: If a stakeholder states 'The software must be incredibly reliable,' which metric is most suitable to validate this once the system is in operation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The total number of lines of code.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).

choices.label: C
choices.text: The number of days since the last marketing campaign.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The average salary of the development team.

questions.explanation: Reliability is quantified by metrics like MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) or MTTF (Mean Time To Failure).
